coredata.dc:title: Potential energy surface of excited semiconductors: Graphene quantum dot and BODIPY
coredata.dc:creator: Colherinhas, Guilherme
Fileti, Eudes Eterno
Chaban, Vitaly V.
coredata.dc:description: Binding energy (BE) is an important descriptor in chemistry, which determines thermodynamics and phase behavior of a given substance. BE between two molecules is not directly accessible from the experiment. It has to be reconstructed from cohesive energies, vaporization heats, etc. We report BE for the excited states of two semiconductor molecules – boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) – with water. We show, for the first time, that excitation increases BE twofold at an optimal separation (energy minimum position), whereas higher separations lead to higher differences. Interestingly, the effects of excitation are similar irrespective of the dominant binding interactions (van der Waals or electrostatic) in the complex. This new knowledge is important for simulations of the excited semiconductors by simplified interaction functions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene
BODIPY
Water
Binding energy
TDDFT
Excited states

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Simulated molecular complexes: (left) H2O@BODIPY and (right) H2O@GQD. The depicted geometries of the complexes were optimized following the evolution of forces acting on each atom.
Selected occupied and unoccupied molecular orbitals of the H2O@BODIPY (left) and H2O@GQD (right) complexes participating in the investigated excited states.

Binding energies keeping the BODIPY molecule and the water molecule together. Comparison between the ground (N =0) and the excited (N =1–5) states. The energies were normalized per electron. The N =4 state is not depicted, since it involves the electronic excitation of the water molecule.
Binding energies keeping the BODIPY molecule and the water molecule together. Differences between the ground state and the excited states are given in percents.
Binding energies keeping the GQD and the water molecule together in the ground (N =0) and excited (N =3–4) states. The energies were normalized per electron.
Differences between the binding energy in the ground state and the binding energy in the excited states of the H2O@GQD complex.
(a) Basis set superposition errors in the simulated systems: (a) H2O@BODIPY; (b) H2O@GQD.

Electronic transition energy, electronic transition wave length, and oscillator strength for five electronic transitions for the H2O@BODIPY and H2O@GQD systems. The table, furthermore, compares the correlation-consistent polarized valence-only double-zeta, cc-pVDZ, and the triple-zeta split-valence polarized, 6-311G*, basis sets.

Coefficients A and B obtained from fitting the BODIPY–water interaction energy curves to theoretical equation
E = - A ×
R
- 6
- B ×
R
- 1
.

head.ce:title: Potential energy surface of excited semiconductors: Graphene quantum dot and BODIPY

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departamento de Física-CEPAE, Universidade Federal de Goiás, 74690-900 Goiânia, Brazil

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Instituto de Ciência e Tecnologia, Universidade Federal de São Paulo, 12231-280 São José dos Campos, SP, Brazil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Excitation of graphene quantum dot significantly alters its interaction with water.

Binding energy (BE) is an important descriptor in chemistry, which determines thermodynamics and phase behavior of a given substance. BE between two molecules is not directly accessible from the experiment. It has to be reconstructed from cohesive energies, vaporization heats, etc. We report BE for the excited states of two semiconductor molecules – boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) and graphene quantum dot (GQD) – with water. We show, for the first time, that excitation increases BE twofold at an optimal separation (energy minimum position), whereas higher separations lead to higher differences. Interestingly, the effects of excitation are similar irrespective of the dominant binding interactions (van der Waals or electrostatic) in the complex. This new knowledge is important for simulations of the excited semiconductors by simplified interaction functions.

ce:section.ce:para: Binding energy (BE) represents an energy that is required to disassemble the object into components. This quantity is closely related to dissociation energy, which is a work to be performed for separating components by a sufficient distance, so that further separation involves a negligible additional work. A molecular or ionic complex typically possesses a lower potential energy than a sum of its constituent parts. BE is typically a negative value in the units of energy, corresponding to complex in vacuum at zero temperature.

Binding non-covalent energy constitutes an important thermodynamic property, which directly affects phase transition temperatures, transport properties, miscibility of liquids, etc. Binding covalent energy is responsible for molecular stability and reactivity. BE is necessary to characterize multiple physical properties, such as adsorption, solvation, reactivity, and ion cluster formation [1–12]. BE is routinely employed to develop and validate interaction models for versatile atomically-precise molecular simulations based on the ab initio calculations [13]. This calibration procedure is known as energy mapping.

BE can be readily computed from total potential energies of counterparts and a total potential energy of a complex. In turn, total potential energies can be derived from ab initio electronic-structure calculations, e.g. using density functional theory (DFT). If an atom-centered basis set is used, the basis set superposition error (BSSE) must be identified and deducted prior to reporting BE. This is usually done through introduction of ghost atoms. Ghost atoms are computational models, which possess basis functions to describe a particular chemical element, but do not possess appropriate nuclei and electrons. The BE calculations are currently widely used to describe new materials and peculiar non-additive interactions [11,14–21].

Denis recently employed the dispersion-corrected DFT to investigate addition of aryl radicals to graphene [22]. It was shown that addition of aryl groups onto defect sites improves the feasibility of the considered reaction. In particular, binding energies per aryl moiety were increased by −27kcalmol−1. The C48H12 graphene flakes were used for the calculations with 50k-points and double-zeta basis set with polarization functions. In another work by this author [23], isolated alkyl radicals were shown to be inefficient for graphene functionalization. A large unfavorable entropic contribution was identified.

Malyi and coworkers [24] reported ab initio molecular dynamics (DFT within the generalized gradient approximation) of sodium adsorption and diffusion on ideal graphene considering Na–Na interactions and dispersion forces. The computed binding energy vs. the vacuum reference state, −0.75eV, appears higher than the cohesive energy of the sodium metal, −1.07eV. However, if the dispersion correction is included, the binding energy, −1.14eV, approaches the sodium metal cohesive energy, −1.21eV. High concentrations of Na result in formation of Na complexes on graphene.

Voloshina and coworkers [25] applied the coupled-cluster method to describe water physisorption on the graphene quantum dot (GQD). The authors claim that the accuracy of DFT calculations is mediocre when applied to the cases, in which van der Waals interactions play a major role, such as water and graphene. It was also shown that the circumflex-like orientation of water is more energetically favorable than the caron-like orientation. Atop adsorption site is preferable, whereas the most stable identified structure of the complex exhibits an adsorption energy of −0.135eV. It has to be noted that the discussed computations used a relatively modest basis set, cc-pVDZ, whereas the post-Hartree–Fock methods are known to be highly sensitive to this factor (in contrast to DFT, for instance). Hydrogen adsorption on graphene and MoS2 nanosheets using different implementations of DFT was actively pursued [1,6,11,26,27].

All the above referenced works employ BE in the ground state of the molecules to characterize the physical phenomena of interest. These phenomena are not limited to inter-molecular binding, but comprise it as an elementary event. In turn, our present work reports the excited-state binding. This phenomenon is both practically and fundamentally interesting for the chemical entities that exhibit medium band gaps. These excited-state BEs are difficult to derive from experiments, and we are not aware of any such work. BEs are important to understand how the excited molecules interact with one another and with the ground-state molecules, e.g. water. These data can be used to derive refined empirical force fields for classical molecular dynamics simulations [28–31]. We report binding between (1) boron-dipyrromethene (BODIPY) and water and (2) GQD and water. BODIPY and GQD are semiconductor molecules, which can be easily excited. In turn, the water molecule exhibits a large band gap. Note the difference between BODIPY and GQD: the first molecule is polar (4.4D) exhibiting a dominant dipole–dipole binding to water, whereas the second molecule is non-polar and its binding to water is of van der Waals nature. Using time-dependent hybrid density functional theory (TD-HDFT), we found that BE in the excited states is systematically higher, as compared to BE in the ground state.

Ground and excited states of the two systems: H2O@BODIPY and H2O@GQD (Fig. 1 ) were considered. The wave function of each system was found by means of the hybrid density functional theory (HDFT). The Becke-3-Lee-Yang-Parr HDFT functional [32,33] with the cc-pVDZ basis set was employed. The convergence criterion upon the wave function optimization was set to 10−6 hartree. The geometries of the complexes were optimized by the Berny algorithm using GEDIIS [34] in the ground (singlet) state. Five excited states were evaluated by TD-HDFT using a computationally efficient scheme [35]. Binding energies were calculated as an energy gain due to a complex formation, Eb
= E 12 − E 1 − E 2 −BSSE, where BSSE stands for the basis set superposition error derived via the counterpoise method. The dispersion correction was applied [36,37]. The potential energy curves were recorded with a step of 0.5Å starting from the lowest-energy separation. The HDFT and TD-HDFT calculations were performed in Gaussian 09D (gaussian.com).

The depicted distances between the interacting molecules correspond to the smallest atom–atom distances after the structures were optimized in the ground state. After geometry relaxation, the separation was changed step-wise and BEs were recorded for the respective excited states. Since orientations of the molecules were obtained as a result of geometry relaxation, the computed BEs are well-defined.

We computed a few excited states to be easily achieved through electronic transitions in the H2O@BODIPY and H2O@GQD systems. Table 1 summarizes general characteristics of the computed excited states, such as transition energy, wave length, oscillator strength, and priority electronic transitions. We also compared different basis sets and found that cc-pVDZ and 6-311G* give results, whose mutual agreement is not ideal, but acceptable (Table 1). Therefore, the cc-pVDZ basis set can be used for our present work to describe relatively large molecules. The numerical parameters of the excited states can be validated through spectroscopic experiments.

The binding energies differ significantly between the ground state and the excited states (Fig. 3 ). Furthermore, the binding energy depends on the electronic state being generally higher for a higher excitation energy. Compare 70calmol−1 per electron in the ground state in the H2O@BODIPY complex to 150calmol−1 in the second excited state with an excitation energy of 3.4699eV and to 130calmol−1 in the third excited state (excitation energy of 3.6615eV). Therefore, excitation of single BODIPY electron from HOMO-1 to LUMO leads to ca. twofold interaction energy increase. Although the observed BE increase is significantly smaller than the corresponding excitation energy, this increase is meaningful for a non-covalent binding phenomenon between neutral molecules. Many physical chemical properties are determined by inter-molecular binding in their condensed phases, such as melting and boiling temperatures, diffusion and viscosity, local structure and mutual miscibility. Excited molecules are present in certain practically important devices, for instance, solar cells. Knowledge about their interactions with other molecules is, therefore, important from the fundamental and practical perspectives. Relative difference between the ground state and the low-lying excited states increases with the distance increase (Fig. 4 ). Since inter-molecular binding generally decreases as
1 /
R
6
, the absolute energy discrepancies decay accordingly.

The largest differences (Fig. 4) were found at larger inter-molecular distances, 0.7–0.8nm, exceeding 3000%. In the case of the fifth excited state, they are even higher, more than 10,000%. The absolute values of BEs are small, though. These observations suggest that potential energy surfaces in the excited states are modified significantly and non-uniformly. Binding of BODIPY and water occurs primarily due to the dipole–dipole interaction. Consider, the dipole moment of BODIPY in the ground state amounts to 4.40D and the dipole moment of water is 1.85D. In the excited states, the dipole moment of BODIPY fluctuates significantly (4.15, 3.96, 3.90, 2.73D for #1, #2, #3 and #5 states, respectively). These values do not correlate well with the binding energies. A better correlation was observed with the dipole moment of the complex (3.34, 9.80, 3.16, 3.21D for #1, #2, #3, and #5 states, respectively). The strongest interaction (150calmol−1) correlates with the highest dipole moment (9.80D).

Excitation does not change the semiconductor–water distance at which most negative BE is observed. Both in the water@BODIPY and water@GQD complexes, this distance amounts to 0.28nm. The well depths are necessary for development of the force fields, being 7.16, 12.52, 15.86, 14.07, 11.80kcalmol−1 in the ground, first, second, third, and fifth excited states of BODIPY, respectively. In water@GQD, the well depths are 1.72, 3.58, 3.27kcalmol−1 in the ground, third, and fourth excited states, respectively.

Nanostructures are known to be inherently polarizable. For instance, this effect was in details characterized in our recent work on the C60 fullerene and ionic liquids [38]. GQD offers a number of vacant p-orbitals, which get populated upon excitation. Since an electronic transition occurs between p-orbitals, it does not change an electronic density distribution within GQD. However, the excited nanostructure is more polarizable and its interaction with polar molecules, such as water, is stronger due to induced electric moments.

Increase of binding energies by two times is an important and poorly predictable effect. Figs. 3 and 5 depict the attraction parts of the interaction curves. In the case of GQD, these data can be approximated by the
E = - A ×
R
- 6
equation, where E is energy, R is distance, A is an empirical coefficient (Table 2 ). In the case of BODIPY, an electrostatic term must be added,
E = - A ×
R
- 6
- B ×
R
- 1
. In view of the well-known Lennard-Jones (12,6) equation,
A = 4 ε
σ
6
, where ε and σ are the Lennard-Jones (12,6) parameters, the depth of potential energy well and the atomic size constant, respectively. The computed coefficients A amount to 3.5, 6.9, 7.2, 6.8 and 5.7 (×10−2 calnm6) for the ground, #1, #2, #3 and #5 excited states of BODIPY, respectively. For GQD, A =6.3×10−3 calnm6 (ground state); A=12×10−3 calnm6 (#3 excited state); 13×10−3 calnm6 (#5 excited state). These differences are significant and must be accounted for when developing interaction models for the excited molecules.

The BSSEs were deducted from the reported binding energies. Fig. 7 compares BSSE for the water@BODIPY complex and the water@GQD complex. Similar to absolute values of the binding energies, BSSE in an order of magnitude smaller for GQD. BSSE decays to negligible values beyond 0.5nm and does not change meaningfully at larger distances. At smaller distances (R <0.5nm), however, BSSE constitutes a significant percentage of the observed BE. BSSE can be, to certain extent, decreased by using larger basis sets. In this work, we had to use moderate basis sets due to significant sizes of the simulated semiconductor molecules.

We used time-dependent density functional theory calculations to simulate excited states of the semiconductor molecules: BODIPY and GQD and their BEs with water. We found that excitation of the semiconductor molecules increases their BEs approximately twofold with the water molecule. We suppose that higher excited states are able to further increase this non-covalent binding by making nanostructures more polarizable. If the d-orbital is attained upon excitation, this will likely result in a qualitatively different binding regularity. Significant excitation energies and intensive irradiation are required. Interestingly, the binding energy increase is similar for BODIPY, which interacts with water electrostatically, and for GQD, which interacts with water via the weak van der Waals forces. The reported observations are fundamentally important. If the excited molecular are simulated using the classical force field, the binding energy increase must be accounted for by certain re-parametrization of the existing potential functions. An ability of excitation to decrease hydrophobicity of GQD may have certain interesting implications for exfoliation and solvation. The reported BEs may present certain practical value for the solar harvesting applications, in which the semiconductor molecules (photon harvesters) get excited by photons of visible and ultraviolet light.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We obtained support from FAPEG, CAPES, FAPESP, and CNPq.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.chemphys.2016.05.011.
